messages.content:
Savey said on 01 December 2023

It appears the BBC One Christmas idents this year are focusing on CBBC show KikoRiki, Good considering the characters in that show are anthropomorphic animals, making them awesome for this time of year, I’d assume they will launch tonight in the junction between Doctor Who and Unbreakable, Doctor Who is their highest rating show, so it makes sense to lead into Unbreakable by launching the Christmas idents, Or they could hold it off till tommorow and launch them at Strictly. Whatever works.

Currently still running with the normal dancers idents (when will they be retired, they’ve been used since 2002)

Emma said on 01 December 2023

Hopefully S4C does something with their new blob mascot, Last year they had TV sets stacked up on top of each other, Then for 10 months they had flickering TV sets, They have finally rebranded and have introduced their first ever mascot: a shape-shifting blob, I hope BBC One rebrand soon, those dancers sure are getting tired, As for the KikoRiki themed Christmas idents, I was told they are launching tonight after Doctor Who, They are being introduced just before Unbreakable.

HouseofFun said on 01 December 2023

Doctor Who ending now, announcement playing “A team of 8 people are pushed to the absolute limit, Unbreakable, our brand new game show is up next, But first, Time to say hello and spend Christmas with the Doctor” I’m not liking this one a whole lot…, They seem to be running a trailer advertising the Doctor Who Christmas special, which revolves around the Doctor having to protect his companions from a man who seeks to find a fob watch (the one The Doctor made himself human with one time), Although just before Doctor Who tonight they had a announcement saying “Christmas is switched on tonight in 1 hour in time for Unbreakable, But first, Is this the end for the Doctor?”. Doesn’t seem like they are advertising the Christmas pres at all,

Edit: Christmas pres featuring KikoRiki switched on, First ident shown features Rosa the Pig doing a dramatic ice skate dance (She even uses eyedrops for fake tears) While this is happening we hear dramatic music, The announcer says “Welcome to Christmas on BBC One, Let us entertain you, But now a team of 8 people are being pushed to their absolute limits, Are they Unbreakable?” Not sure why they didn’t tell us it was happening untill it did, But still a gorgeous ident, I noticed when the Announcer started talking it sounded as if he was driven to tears by the beauty of Rosa’s gorgeous dance. The best thing to come out of this package since the “Lights” christmas idents in 2006,

Eyes said on 01 December 2023

A beautiful ident, Best thing to come out of the “Rythm and Movement” package since those Planet Earth 3 special idents a few months ago, i feel sad that KikoRiki has not been shown much on CBBC on BBC Two, but I’m glad it’s representing the Xmas idents on BBC One (Although I also wish the idents also featured a merman).

Maile said on 01 December 2023

Best Christmas ident in a while, I just love that they have spent it with Rosa and her friends, I hear there is also one featuring Krash the Rabbit opening his presents, I like how they used the usual ident theme (dancing) in that first ident, KikoRiki looks like it’s gonna be good this year, but I hope they do more with the style (and maybe finally retire Rythm and Movement idents)

Faramad said on 01 December 2023

News safe ident just on, Basically just a Christmas tree on a snowy background, with a music box Christmas carol playing, on top of that the only thing we see is a sleeping Rosa (We vaguely hear her snoring) Glad they are now using characters in their news safe idents, Announcer said “Now on BBC One, time for the BBC News at 8”. Remember the Lights idents the used just before overhauling their promo style in late 2006/early 2007? That was pretty cool, but this is way better. I seem to remember those idents started on a dark street but Christmas lights started turning on before it got to a city center and then people were dancing, It was used again in 2007, And now it seems like the “Project Chameleon” endboard template introduced in July is out the window, at least for now, replaced with a snowy background with a Christmas tree on it. Signs that Rythm and Movement is finally working towards the end.

KikoRiki said on 01 December 2023

We’re glad you love our cartoon and the special BBC One idents featuring our characters,

However, Rosa will be the star, (We will have idents revolving around Chiko, Krash and the others), As to what you said, BBC One are looking into introducing the Circle idents already in use in other countries, so don’t expect to see Ballet, Capoeria or Festival/Rave back in the new year. Get used to Kites, Capes, Hippos and Bikes as that’s what might be coming after our Christmas campaign, We don’t know for certain, but the Dancers might not be coming back after Christmas

WhovianForever: Did everyone catch the Doctor Who Christmas special announcement during the news? I’m so hyped! Can’t wait to see how the Doctor deals with the fob watch saga this holiday season!

LemonyFan: And how about they’re showing the 2004 ‘A Series of Unfortunate Events’ movie!! That’s one of my holiday favorites; the nostalgia is real. 😍🍿

ChristmasCarol: Truly is shaping up to be a wonderful holiday schedule on BBC One. Between Doctor Who and the classic movies, I’m going to be glued to my screen with all the mulled wine and mince pies!

KikoFanatic: Plus, the KikoRiki Christmas idents have been adorable so far! Rosa’s ice skate dance nearly brought tears to my eyes just like the announcer. 🐷⛸ Christmas is definitely switched on!

RetroTV: I’m just here wondering if the new ‘Circle’ idents KikoRiki hinted at is a sign we’re finally saying goodbye to the “Rythm and Movement” package for good. After 20+ years, it’s about time for a major refresh!

NostalgiaNerd: @RetroTV I’m with you on that. The Lights idents back in '06/'07 were beautiful. It’s cool to see they’re still willing to shake things up visually with the news safe ident this year. Sleepy Rosa might just be my new favorite. 😴🎄

SeriesBinger: Doctor Who special + A Series of Unfortunate Events + KikoRiki idents = Best Christmas TV lineup or what? Also, hope BBC One throws in some classic Christmas episodes of our favorite shows. Maybe some ‘Vicar of Dibley’ or ‘Only Fools and Horses’?

CircleCurious: Anyone heard more about these new Circle idents? I love change, and KikoRiki characters have been great, but I’m already excited for what’s coming next. Bring on the new era!

KikoLover: Just saw a new Krash and Chiko ident! They’re having a blast playing in the snow! ❄️🐇 So cute watching Krash throw snowballs while Chiko dodges around. Really captures the winter fun vibes.

SciFiSavvy: Speaking of new idents, did anyone else notice the Dokko the Moose building a laser device ident? It aired right before ‘Doctor Who’ and set the PERFECT tone for sci-fi. Dokko’s always been the tech genius of the group. 🦌🔧

HedgehogAdmirer: @KikoLover Chiko is always adorable, but seeing him in the snow was next level! BBC One knows how to do Christmas idents right. Plus, the animation on these characters in the idents is super impressive this year.

WhovianGuru: @SciFiSavvy Loved the Dokko ident! It’s like a mini episode of ‘Doctor Who’ in itself. Brilliant tie-in to the show’s theme! Makes waiting for the Christmas special even more exciting.

BakeMaster: But guys, let’s talk about the Olga the Owl baking ident. The detail on that gingerbread house she was making! I can almost smell the cookies through the screen. 🦉🍪 It’s the cozy ident I never knew I needed in my life.

HolidayWatcher: Right?! The Olga ident feels like being wrapped in a warm blanket with a hot chocolate in hand. It’s chef’s kiss perfection. BBC One’s going all out with these KikoRiki characters for Christmas.

DoctorFan: Dokko’s ident was like a mini teaser for what’s coming with ‘Doctor Who’. Immersive and full of that tech mystery vibe. I appreciate that BBC One creates these unique intros for thematic continuity.

AnimationAppreciator: Major kudos to the animation team for the KikoRiki idents. As someone who dabbles in animation, the quality is sensational. It’s like a little holiday gift before each show starts!

ChristmasCheer: Can’t get enough of the snow play with Krash and Chiko. It’s absolutely delightful and spreads so much joy. These little moments provided by the idents add a special touch to the holiday season.

SciCompanion: Dokko and his inventions always remind me that anything’s possible with a little imagination. Such an inspiring ident to lead into ‘Doctor Who’. Well played, BBC One! Can’t wait to see what else is in store, I’m gonna miss these idents when new year comes around! 😢

WelcomeToSciFi: The Dokko centric ident really sets the scene for what’s to come on ‘Doctor Who’ this Christmas. One of the best things about the KikoRiki set, Far better than the usual dancers, Come to think of it, they have been using the dancers since 2002, so now is a good time for a revamp, I guess 😉

Olgo5: Just saw the Olga ident and damn, Olga really is a talented chef, They always seem to show it before cooking shows, Which ident normally leads into Eastenders, It seems to vary between Krash and Chiko in the snow, Olga cooking and Rosa ice skating. Not sure how this is consistent, But that Rosa one brings tears to my eyes with her graceful skating, Not sure why the Dokko one only plays before Doctor Who, Maybe because it fits it well

Prolouge:

User1: So, what’s everyone thinking BBC One’s going to pull out of the hat for this year’s Christmas idents? Last year was decent, but I’m hoping for something with a bit more pizzazz.

WhovianForever: I’m crossing my fingers for something Doctor Who-themed! With David Tennant back in the show earlier this year, it would be perfect timing to capitalize on that for the festive season. Can you imagine an ident with the TARDIS all decked out in Christmas lights?

HolidayJoy: That would be cool for us Whovians, for sure, but BBC One usually goes for something that appeals to everyone. Doctor Who is big, but not universally loved by every single viewer. Plus, they tend to lean towards more generic festive symbols.

SeriesBinger: True, they’ll probably aim for something more inclusive. Like a cozy winter scene or playful animation. Maybe bring back some classic characters or even partner with a popular children’s show this time around for wider appeal?

RetroTV: I’ve got a hunch they’ll go with animation this year. It has that universal charm, right? Plus, it would work perfectly with both the kids’ content and the family-friendly vibe they love during Christmas.

User1: That’s an interesting angle. They could get quite creative with animation. Plus, they’re probably due for a change since the “Rythm and Movement” dancers have been around since 2002.

KikoFanatic: What if they teamed up with a show like KikoRiki? The characters are fun, visually engaging, and it’s something that would stand out. Plus, they could do loads with those quirky little animals.

LemonyFan: KikoRiki would be such a cute choice! It could be a hit with children and adults alike if done right. And it’s refreshingly different from usual idents, without relying on something niche like a specific Doctor Who theme.

WhovianForever: Okay, I see your point. Much as I’d love Doctor Who idents, something like KikoRiki could offer that ‘Christmas with the family’ vibe they probably want. I just hope they add a little extra magic this year, you know?

CircleCurious: Whatever they do, I hope it’s something new. We’ve had the “Rythm and Movement” package idents for such a long time. It’s high time for something fresh and exciting to take us into the new year! Write a forum convo saying that the background used for the technical fault/show not avilailable on this platform/BBC One HD can’t show programmes from this area, is Rosa’s legs sticking out of icy waters, This means that for now, the purple/pink/white gradient used for the not available on this platform/BBC One HD can’t show programmes from this area background is out of use
TechTVGuru: Hey folks, have you all noticed the new background BBC One is using for those “technical fault/show not available on this platform/BBC One HD can’t show programmes from this area” screens? It’s quite a departure from the usual purple/pink/white gradient we’ve become accustomed to.

VisualMediaFan: Really? What did they switch it to? I haven’t caught it yet.

TechTVGuru: It’s actually quite clever and in line with this year’s Christmas theme. They’ve gone for an image of Rosa’s legs sticking out of icy waters. It’s a whimsical yet subtle nod to the KikoRiki Christmas idents they’re running this season.

AnimationAdmirer: That sounds hilarious! 😂 BBC One is really integrating KikoRiki into everything this Christmas, aren’t they? I love how they find ways to weave the theme across different areas, even in the technical backgrounds.

ScreenSavvy: Gotta admire the creativity. Out with the old gradient and in with something that has a bit more charm and personality. But using Rosa’s legs, of all things—it’s a bold move. Does it work well in practice?

VisualMediaFan: It definitely grabs your attention. And it’s nice to see something different for a change. That said, I wonder if everyone gets that it’s supposed to be playful, given it’s for a technical fault message or region restriction.

TechTVGuru: True, but it’s such a quirky touch. Probably gives a little chuckle rather than the annoyance you’d feel with a standard error screen. Plus, it keeps the festive spirit alive, even when things aren’t going as planned.

ErrorScreenEnthusiast: I must say, BBC One is becoming quite daring with their presentation. From the sound of it, Rosa’s legs are a memorable choice. Makes you almost hope for a technical hiccup just to see it. Almost. 😄

BroadcastingBuff: Do you think this indicates a more playful and adventurous direction for BBC One’s branding in general? Or is it just a seasonal flourish?

TechTVGuru: Hard to say for sure, but it could signify a willingness to step outside the traditional boundaries, at least during the holiday season. Whether this carries over into other areas of their branding or presentation remains to be seen. I, for one, hope the creativity keeps up!

VisualMediaFan: Same here. It’s refreshing to see them play around and be a bit more whimsical. Makes the whole experience of watching BBC One more fun, even when you’re just staring at a technical fault screen.

RetroNostalgiaLover: Remember the days when a technical fault screen was just a boring logo or text message? It’s nice to see some evolution. Who knew we’d get to a point where we’re discussing the artistic merit of a technical fault background? 😆

TechTVGuru: Progress and creativity in the most unexpected places! Let’s see if this sparks a trend across other channels too. But for now, hats off to BBC One for making even the technical glitches a part of the festive cheer.
Write a convo where we see it, and here’s a recap, Andrew Marr was getting very angry with a guest, and then started interrupting and BERATING her over her political opinion, Then he gets carried off the set yelling and then Poof, breakdown/technical fault caption

TechWatcher: Did anyone catch that bizarre moment on BBC One during the Andrew Marr interview? Things escalated quickly!

NewsJunkie: Yes! Marr completely lost his temper with the guest, interrupting and berating her over her political stance. It was unlike anything I've seen on his show before.

DramaLover: And then, as if it couldn't get any weirder, security literally had to carry him off the set while he was still yelling! Never expected to see something like that live.

TechWatcher: Exactly! And right after that chaos, the screen went to the technical fault caption. But instead of the usual message, it was Rosa's legs sticking out of icy waters. BBC One's creativity at its finest, even during a breakdown.

ScreenSavvy: I thought the Rosa legs screen was a funny touch, considering the circumstances. It made the unexpected interruption a bit more bearable.

VisualMediaFan: Agreed. In any other context, the humor might have been lost, but after such an intense moment, it was a welcome distraction. Plus, it kept the KikoRiki Christmas theme going strong.

ErrorScreenEnthusiast: I almost couldn’t believe what I was watching. Marr getting dragged off and then cutting to Rosa’s legs? It’s like live TV gold. You just can’t predict these moments.

BroadcastingBuff: This incident might just go down in history. Marr’s outburst, followed by a playful error message? BBC One managed to turn a potentially negative situation into something memorably quirky.

NewsJunkie: Do you think they planned to use the Rosa legs screen for all technical faults during the Christmas period, or was it a spur-of-the-moment decision given the content just before it?

TechWatcher: I bet it’s part of their holiday theme setup, but its use here was perfectly timed. Makes you wonder if they have a sense of humor about these emergencies, planning for a light-hearted touch regardless of the reason.

DramaLover: Whether planned or not, it showcased the unpredictable nature of live television and how even moments of breakdown can be turned into something engaging for the viewers.

ScreenSavvy: After this, the standard technical fault screens are going to feel so dull in comparison. BBC One has set a new bar for handling on-air mishaps with a dash of humor.

VisualMediaFan: Hopefully, this encourages more channels to rethink how they present technical difficulties. A little creativity can turn an interruption into an unexpected highlight.
Write a follow up wondering if it's a still image or animated (like the usual gradient used during football rights restrictions/bbc one HD cant show this local show)

TechWatcher: After the wild moment with Andrew Marr and the surprise Rosa legs technical fault screen, it got me thinking—was that just a still image, or do you think BBC One went the extra mile with some animation?

ScreenSavvy: Good question. Their usual gradient during football rights restrictions or when BBC One HD can't show a local program tends to be animated, even if just slightly. It would be interesting to see if they've animated Rosa's legs somehow.

VisualMediaFan: Imagine if they had Rosa’s legs gently kicking or creating little ripples in the icy water. It would add a whole extra layer of detail and creativity to their already unique technical fault message.

ErrorScreenEnthusiast: That would be hilarious and brilliantly unexpected! In the context of what had just happened on-air, some light animation would be a delightful contrast. Has anyone noticed any movement, or was it too brief?

DramaLover: I only saw it for a moment, but it looked like a still image to me. Even so, the idea of incorporating some subtle animation doesn’t seem too far off for BBC One, given their current creative streak.

BroadcastingBuff: If they haven't animated it yet, they should consider it. BBC One's technical fault screens have the potential to become mini-events in themselves, especially with how much attention the Rosa legs screen has already garnered.

NewsJunkie: True. An animated technical fault screen, especially with something as quirky as Rosa’s legs sticking out of icy waters, would be memorable. It could lighten the mood during technical difficulties or broadcasting hiccups.

TechWatcher: Absolutely. It's moments like these that can set a network apart in terms of how they handle unpredictability. Animation would not only align with the holiday theme but also keep viewers engaged, even when things go awry.

ScreenSavvy: Plus, considering the disruption from the Andrew Marr incident, having something light-hearted and possibly animated might have been just what was needed to defuse the tension.

VisualMediaFan: Either way, I'm just impressed with how BBC One is handling their Christmas theme and even incorporating it into the unexpected moments. Here’s hoping they reveal whether Rosa’s icy dip was a one-off or if there's more playful animation lying in wait for the next technical glitch.

ErrorScreenEnthusiast: Definitely keeping an eye out now. Between the potential of animated error screens and the usual holiday broadcasts, BBC One seems to be making this festive season particularly noteworthy.
Write a follow up where it appears and we get our answer if its still/silent or animated with sound, as BBC One Wales can't show football on Iplayer or Sky

TechWatcher: Well, folks, we got our answer about the BBC One technical fault screen featuring Rosa from KikoRiki during today's football game. Since BBC One Wales couldn’t show the match on iPlayer or Sky, we saw the technical fault screen again. And guess what? It was indeed animated, with sound!

ScreenSavvy: Really? That's fantastic! What kind of animation and sound are we talking? Was Rosa doing anything particularly funny or cute?

VisualMediaFan: The animation was subtle but effective. Rosa’s legs were gently kicking, causing ripples to spread across the icy water surface. As for the sound, there were faint, whimsical splashes that accompanied the movements. It was quite the charming touch, keeping viewers entertained even amidst the frustration of not being able to watch the football match.

ErrorScreenEnthusiast: That sounds adorable and so creative! It’s not every day you see a network using their technical fault screen as an opportunity for a little storytelling. The sound effects must have added a nice layer of immersion.

DramaLover: So, it wasn’t just a still image after all. BBC One is really stepping up their game with these holiday-themed idents and fault screens. It’s a clever way to maintain viewer engagement, even when broadcasting rights issues crop up.

BroadcastingBuff: Agreed. It’s a smart move, especially considering the often unexpected nature of broadcasting rights restrictions. This sort of creative thinking shows they’re considering the viewer's experience at every turn, even during less-than-ideal circumstances.

NewsJunkie: Plus, it's fitting with the overall festive and playful vibe of this year’s Christmas programming. It’s these little details that can turn a potentially negative viewer experience (like missing a football match) into something memorable and positive.

TechWatcher: Absolutely. It's a testament to BBC One's innovative approach to broadcasting and viewer engagement. Keeping the theme consistent across all platforms, even down to the technical fault screens, enhances the overall brand experience.

ScreenSavvy: I’m impressed by the creativity and effort to make even the mandatory technical fault screen something viewers can enjoy. Makes you wonder what other surprises BBC One might have in store for us this holiday season.

VisualMediaFan: Indeed. With this level of attention to detail, I'm looking forward to what else they've planned. It certainly sets a high bar for holiday broadcasts, proving that even the smallest elements can contribute to a larger festive atmosphere.

ErrorScreenEnthusiast: Here’s hoping other networks take note and add a bit of creativity to their technical or rights restriction screens. It’s a small touch, but clearly, it can make a big difference in how interruptions are perceived by the audience.

DramaLover: Cheers to BBC One for finding a way to spread holiday cheer, even in the most unlikely of places. I'll be keeping my eyes peeled for more animated surprises throughout the season.

Write that there was her muffled voice, and small bubbles rising before the announcer said he'll take it from here and said "This Sport programme is not available on this service, but if you're viewing in Wales, please tune to BBC One Wales on your digital TV, We sincerely apologise for any inconvenience caused and thank you for your understanding" This event happens every 6 minutes in the loop

TechTrendSpotter: Did anyone else catch the full extent of the animated Rosa technical fault screen during the football broadcast blackout on BBC One Wales online services? Not only was it animated with Rosa’s legs gently kicking, but there was also her muffled voice and small bubbles rising to the surface of the water. It was both whimsical and incredibly detailed.

DigitalViewer: Yes, I saw that! The addition of Rosa’s muffled voice and the bubbles really brought the whole scene to life. It was a nice touch that made the wait more bearable. And every couple of minutes, the announcer would come in with a polite reminder about the sport programme not being available and directing viewers in Wales to tune to BBC One Wales on digital TV. 

AnimationFan123: It’s fascinating how they managed to keep it engaging over the loop. Every 6 minutes, the animation would reset, and the announcer would repeat his message. Despite the repetition, the charm of the animation and the considerate tone of the announcer somehow kept the frustration of missing the live football to a minimum.

SportsEnthusiast: I appreciated the announcer’s professionalism and the clarity of the instructions on how to find the match on digital TV for those in Wales. It was a smooth way to handle what could have been a confusing situation for some viewers. Plus, the Rosa scene added a light-hearted element to the mix.

ViewerExperienceGuru: This is a prime example of how crucial viewer experience is, even during technical faults or rights restrictions. BBC One turned a potentially negative viewer interaction into a memorable moment that kept the brand's personality consistent and engaging. 

TechTrendSpotter: Definitely. It's the details that make all the difference. The loop being set at every 6 minutes gave viewers enough space between announcements to not feel bombarded but kept it frequent enough to be helpful for anyone just tuning in.

SoundDesignLover: Didn’t anyone else love the sound design? The muffled voice and bubble sounds were such a delightful touch. It’s not often that you get such attention to detail in what is essentially a placeholder screen.

DigitalViewer: Agreed, the sound design was spot on. It turned what could have been a simple loop into something far more engaging. With every iteration, I found myself paying attention, waiting for the announcement and enjoying Rosa’s underwater antics.

AnimationFan123: It’s clever strategies like this that elevate a broadcaster in the eyes of their viewers. Engaging content doesn’t have to stop just because of technical restrictions. BBC One really set a benchmark with this approach.

SportsEnthusiast: Absolutely, turning a limitation into an opportunity for creativity is something all networks should take note of. It speaks volumes about BBC One's commitment to viewer satisfaction and their ability to innovate within any constraints.

Write a follow up about the version in use during local news on BBC One HD, which also has the announcer interrupt her trying to say it while only her legs are above surface, and then the announcer explaining that BBC One HD can't show programmes from your area

TechTrendWatcher: Just caught an interesting variation of the BBC One HD technical fault screen during the time slated for local news. Like the version seen during the football broadcast blackout, this one featured Rosa from KikoRiki, but with a twist tailored for the HD channel's inability to show local programming.

HDViewerPro: Right, I noticed that too. It was a clever spin on the theme, showing only Rosa’s legs above the surface. But before she could "say" anything through her muffled voice and bubbles, the announcer quickly interrupted, smoothly taking over the situation.

MediaAnalyst: Exactly, the announcer's timely intervention before Rosa could "speak" was a humorous touch. He went on to explain that BBC One HD couldn’t show programmes from the viewer’s area, guiding them on what to do instead. It kept the continuity of Rosa's underwater adventures while serving the practical purpose of informing the audience about the local news blackout on HD.

DigitalBroadcastFan: I thought the way they integrated the announcer's message was pretty seamless. Keeping the amusing visual of Rosa struggling to communicate, then having the announcer clarify the situation, added a light-hearted element to what might otherwise be a frustrating viewer experience.

ViewerExperienceAdvocate: This adaptation for the local news slot on BBC One HD exemplifies how a broadcasting network can maintain viewer engagement and provide necessary information without losing the entertainment value, even in the context of technical restrictions or channel limitations.

SoundWaveEnthusiast: And did anyone else catch how the sound design played into this version? Rosa’s effort to communicate, followed by the announcer's voice, was not only clear but also kept the playful tone consistent. The blend of visuals and audio here makes waiting through these moments more enjoyable than one might expect.

HDTechObserver: It’s a smart move by BBC One, especially considering the confusion that can arise with local content being unavailable on HD channels. By turning this moment into an extension of their creative Christmas campaign, they not only address the issue directly but do so in a way that’s memorable and engaging.

EntertainmentInsider: Indeed, this approach hints at a broader strategy to enhance the overall brand experience. Even limitations like broadcasting restrictions become opportunities for BBC One to reinforce its commitment to innovative viewer engagement across all its platforms.

LocalNewsFanatic: While the explanation for why BBC One HD can't show local programming was helpful, the inventive use of Rosa’s character made the message more digestible. It definitely softens the blow of not being able to watch my local news in HD.

TechTrendWatcher: This just shows the power of creative problem-solving in broadcasting. By infusing humor and creativity into technical announcements, BBC One effectively turns potential viewer dissatisfaction into an entertaining and informative experience.

Write a follow up where it is made into an ident where she falls over and sumberges then her legs rise up, followed by a quick announcement that "The following programme is not available on some services" or "For local news, please tune to SD" before it fades into the loop, There is also a version shown at the end of the blacked out show that shows her legs vanishing underneath, her being completely gone for 4 seconds then her face resurfacing, catching her breath,

TechVisionary: Have you all seen the latest iteration of the BBC One technical fault screen that has now been creatively adapted into an ident? It’s a fascinating evolution from what we've previously discussed. This time, Rosa dramatically falls into the water, submerges completely, and then her legs rise up to the surface in a comedic fashion. It’s immediately followed by a quick, yet informative, announcement stating, "The following programme is not available on some services" or "For local news, please tune to SD." This seamless transition into the loop keeps viewers both informed and entertained.

DigitalBroadcastEnt: Yes, I caught that! The narrative they’ve managed to weave into these few seconds is both engaging and humorous. It’s a delightful way to tackle the unavoidable issue of service restrictions. Plus, the quick announcement is succinct but delivers all the necessary info without interrupting the charm of the ident.

SoundAndVisionFan: The sound design in this version adds so much to the experience as well. The splash as Rosa falls in, the subtle underwater ambiance, and her legs flailing above - it's all brilliantly executed. Then the clarity of the announcement cuts through, ensuring the message is heard. It's these details that make the ident stand out.

TechCommsGeek: Interestingly, there’s also a version shown at the end of the blacked-out show, which seems to complete the narrative arc. Rosa's legs vanish beneath the surface, leaving the screen quiet and still for a moment. After about 4 seconds of suspense, we see her face resurface, catching her breath in a rather dramatic and humorous fashion. It’s a clever way to ‘wrap up’ the interlude caused by the programming blackout.

ViewerExperienceAnalyst: This narrative approach to what could have been a mundane or even frustrating interruption in programming is strategic. It transforms a technical necessity into a moment of levity and brand engagement. Ensuring viewers are informed while also entertained is an art, and BBC One seems to be mastering it with these idents.

CreativeBroadcastFan: I’m fascinated by how BBC One has personified Rosa in these scenarios, making her almost a mascot for these technical constraints. It’s a smart move that likely increases viewer affinity for the channel's brand. The storytelling aspect ensures that even a technical fault screen or service announcement becomes a memorable part of the viewing experience.

BroadcastTrendAnalyst: Indeed, these narrative-rich idents play a significant role in modern broadcasting by turning potential viewer discontent into an opportunity for engagement. It’ll be interesting to see if this approach influences how other networks handle similar situations._ABCOne has certainly set a high bar for creativity and viewer communication during technical issues.

DigitalMediaStrategist: The duality of using these idents both as informative tools and as extensions of the holiday campaign is ingenious. It reflects a deep understanding of audience expectations and a commitment to enhancing the viewer experience, even during less-than-ideal broadcasting moments.

TechVisionary: It’s these innovations in broadcasting that keep the medium vibrant and engaging. As audiences become more sophisticated and demanding, creative solutions like Rosa's underwater adventures will be what sets channels apart, turning every moment, even technical faults, into opportunities for branding and viewer connection.
Write a convo on 1st January (the normal Dancers are back for now, untill June), and a user noted that the gradient background is not back, and say what has persisted

TechTrendsWatcher: Happy New Year, everyone! Did anyone catch BBC One today? Looks like the normal Dancers idents are back in play. Though, it seems they're only here until June based on what we've heard.

VisualMediaAficionado: Yes, I noticed that too. What caught my attention, though, is the absence of the usual gradient background we’ve become accustomed to. It appears they haven’t reverted back to it after the festive season’s creative idents and error messages.

PixelPerfectPro: Interestingly enough, what has persisted is the use of more dynamic backgrounds across the board. Even without the gradient, there’s a clear evolution in their visual approach. Seems like the creativity sparked by the holiday season is lingering in their aesthetic choices.

BroadcastingBuff: That’s a solid observation. It’s almost as if the holiday season served as a testing ground for BBC One to explore more vibrant and dynamic visuals. It wouldn’t surprise me if this is part of a gradual buildup to whatever new branding they plan to introduce in June.
